basic.title: Basic Chemistry
basic.overview: This route introduces the first families of compounds: water, carbon oxides, ammonia, salts, and simple metal or sulfur oxides.

levels.theory: Water is one of the first stable molecules students meet. It shows how two hydrogens can connect around one oxygen to form a simple but essential structure.
levels.mechanicSummary: Place atoms on the board, connect them with single bonds, and match the target molecule pattern.

levels.theory: Carbon and oxygen can join in different ratios. This lesson introduces the smallest carbon oxide and teaches that the same elements can build different substances.
levels.mechanicSummary: Use the same board-building mechanic, but now compare how one carbon behaves when combined with oxygen.

levels.theory: Carbon dioxide extends the previous idea by adding another oxygen. It helps learners notice how changing atom counts changes the final compound and its structure.
levels.mechanicSummary: Build the next carbon oxide by arranging one carbon with two oxygen atoms in the correct connection pattern.

levels.theory: Ammonia introduces nitrogen as a central atom that can connect to several hydrogens. It is a good first example of a different central-node pattern.
levels.mechanicSummary: Continue using bond placement, but focus on a new center atom with a three-connection pattern.

levels.theory: Methane is one of the simplest carbon-based molecules. It shows carbon as a strong central builder that can connect to multiple hydrogens.
levels.mechanicSummary: Practice a hub-style structure where one carbon becomes the main anchor for the full molecule.

levels.theory: Hydrochloric acid introduces chlorine as a halogen partner. This lesson expands the route from water and oxides into acid-like combinations.
levels.mechanicSummary: Use a simpler two-atom pattern and learn how a new element changes the chemistry route.

levels.theory: Salt is one of the most recognizable compounds in beginner chemistry. It introduces the idea of metal and nonmetal combinations in a very familiar form.
levels.mechanicSummary: Build a basic metal-nonmetal pair and compare it with the acid lesson before it.

levels.theory: Calcium oxide moves the route into basic metal oxides. It helps learners compare how oxygen combines with a different type of element.
levels.mechanicSummary: Keep using the same spatial bond mechanic, but now apply it to a metal oxide family.

levels.theory: Iron oxide lessons introduce transition metals in a simplified form. This first step starts with the smaller oxide before moving to a more complex iron compound.
levels.mechanicSummary: Build a compact oxide structure and prepare for a larger iron pattern in the next lesson.

levels.theory: Iron(III) oxide scales the same family into a larger structure. It shows that one chemistry topic can expand into more complex patterns without changing the mechanic.
levels.mechanicSummary: Use the same connection mechanic, but manage a denser structure with more atoms on the board.

levels.theory: Sulfur dioxide introduces sulfur oxides and reactive gas compounds. It is the first step in a small sulfur branch of the route.
levels.mechanicSummary: Build a sulfur-centered structure and compare it with earlier oxygen-based compounds.

levels.theory: Sulfur trioxide extends the sulfur branch and closes the basic route with a larger oxide. It reinforces the idea that one topic can develop across multiple lessons.
levels.mechanicSummary: Finish the route with a more advanced sulfur oxide that still uses the same core connection mechanic.
